question:
For a positive integer $n$ and any real number $c$, define
$x_k$ recursively by $x_0=0$, $x_1=1$, and for $k\geq 0$,
\[x_{k+2}=\frac{cx_{k+1}-(n-k)x_k}{k+1}.\]
Fix $n$ and then take $c$ to be the largest value for which $x_{n+1}=0$.
Find $x_k$ in terms of $n$ and $k$, $1\leq k\leq n$.
solution:
Clearly $x_{n+1}$ is a polynomial in $c$ of degree $n$,
so it suffices to identify $n$ values of $c$ for which $x_{n+1} =
0$. We claim these are $c = n-1-2r$ for $r=0,1,\dots, n-1$; in this
case, $x_k$ is the coefficient of $t^{k-1}$ in the polynomial
$f(t) = (1-t)^r (1+t)^{n-1-r}$. This can be verified by noticing that
$f$ satisfies the differential equation
\[
\frac{f'(t)}{f(t)} = \frac{n-1-r}{1+t} - \frac{r}{1-t}
\]
(by logarithmic differentiation) or equivalently,
\begin{align*}
(1-t^2) f'(t) &= f(t) [(n-1-r)(1-t) - r(1+t)] \\
&= f(t) [(n-1-2r)  - (n-1)t]
\end{align*}
and then taking the coefficient of $t^{k}$ on both sides:
\begin{gather*}
(k+1) x_{k+2} - (k-1) x_k = \\
(n-1-2r) x_{k+1} - (n-1) x_{k}.
\end{gather*}
In particular, the largest such $c$ is $n-1$, and $x_k =
\binom{n-1}{k-1}$ for $k= 1, 2, \dots, n$.

Greg Kuperberg has suggested an alternate approach to show directly
that $c=n-1$ is the largest root, without computing the others. Note
that the condition $x_{n+1} = 0$ states that $(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ is an
eigenvector of the matrix
\[
A_{ij} = \left\{ \begin{array}{cc} i & j = i + 1 \\ n-j & j=i-1 \\
0&\mbox{otherwise} \end{array} \right.
\]
with eigenvalue $c$. By the Perron-Frobenius theorem, $A$ has a unique
eigenvector with positive entries, whose eigenvalue has modulus
greater than or equal to that of any other eigenvalue, which proves
the claim.

descriptive_long.question:
For a positive integer $sizeparam$ and any real number $coeffparam$, define
$indexedvalue$ recursively by $zerothvalue=0$, $firstvalue=1$, and for $indexer\geq 0$,
\[secondnext=\frac{coeffparam\,successor-(sizeparam-indexer)indexedvalue}{indexer+1}.\]
Fix $sizeparam$ and then take $coeffparam$ to be the largest value for which $afterlast=0$.
Find $indexedvalue$ in terms of $sizeparam$ and $indexer$, $1\leq indexer\leq sizeparam$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Clearly $afterlast$ is a polynomial in $coeffparam$ of degree $sizeparam$,
so it suffices to identify $sizeparam$ values of $coeffparam$ for which $afterlast =
0$. We claim these are $coeffparam = sizeparam-1-2rootindex$ for $rootindex=0,1,\dots, sizeparam-1$; in this
case, $indexedvalue$ is the coefficient of $auxvar^{indexer-1}$ in the polynomial
$polyfunc(auxvar) = (1-auxvar)^{rootindex} (1+auxvar)^{sizeparam-1-rootindex}$. This can be verified by noticing that
$polyfunc$ satisfies the differential equation
\[ rac{polyfunc'(auxvar)}{polyfunc(auxvar)} =  rac{sizeparam-1-rootindex}{1+auxvar} -  rac{rootindex}{1-auxvar}\]
(by logarithmic differentiation) or equivalently,
 egin{align*}
(1-auxvar^2) polyfunc'(auxvar) &= polyfunc(auxvar) [(sizeparam-1-rootindex)(1-auxvar) - rootindex(1+auxvar)] \&= polyfunc(auxvar) [(sizeparam-1-2rootindex)  - (sizeparam-1)auxvar]
\end{align*}
and then taking the coefficient of $auxvar^{indexer}$ on both sides:
 egin{gather*}
(indexer+1) secondnext - (indexer-1) indexedvalue = \ (sizeparam-1-2rootindex) successor - (sizeparam-1) indexedvalue.
\end{gather*}
In particular, the largest such $coeffparam$ is $sizeparam-1$, and $indexedvalue =
 inom{sizeparam-1}{indexer-1}$ for $indexer= 1, 2, \dots, sizeparam$.

Greg Kuperberg has suggested an alternate approach to show directly
that $coeffparam=sizeparam-1$ is the largest root, without computing the others. Note
that the condition $afterlast = 0$ states that $(firstvalue, \dots, lastvalue)$ is an
eigenvector of the matrix
\[matrixentry = \left\{  egin{array}{cc} rowindex & colindex = rowindex + 1 \ sizeparam-colindex & colindex=rowindex-1 \ 0&\mbox{otherwise} \end{array} ight.\]
with eigenvalue $coeffparam$. By the Perron-Frobenius theorem, $A$ has a unique
eigenvector with positive entries, whose eigenvalue has modulus
greater than or equal to that of any other eigenvalue, which proves
the claim.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For a positive integer $passenger$ and any real number $moonshine$, define
$fireplace$ recursively by $trebuchet=0$, $starfruit=1$, and for $driftwood\geq 0$,
\[sugarcane=\frac{moonshine\,motorcycle-(passenger-driftwood)fireplace}{driftwood+1}.\]
Fix $passenger$ and then take $moonshine$ to be the largest value for which $blacksmith=0$.
Find $fireplace$ in terms of $passenger$ and $driftwood$, $1\leq driftwood\leq passenger$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Clearly $blacksmith$ is a polynomial in $moonshine$ of degree $passenger$, so it suffices to identify $passenger$ values of $moonshine$ for which $blacksmith = 0$. We claim these are $moonshine = passenger-1-2woodpecker$ for $woodpecker=0,1,\dots, passenger-1$; in this case, $fireplace$ is the coefficient of $pineapple^{driftwood-1}$ in the polynomial
$buttercup(pineapple) = (1-pineapple)^{woodpecker} (1+pineapple)^{passenger-1-woodpecker}$. This can be verified by noticing that $buttercup$ satisfies the differential equation
\[\frac{buttercup'(pineapple)}{buttercup(pineapple)} = \frac{passenger-1-woodpecker}{1+pineapple} - \frac{woodpecker}{1-pineapple}\]
(by logarithmic differentiation) or equivalently,
\begin{align*}
(1-pineapple^2) buttercup'(pineapple) &= buttercup(pineapple) [(passenger-1-woodpecker)(1-pineapple) - woodpecker(1+pineapple)] \\ &= buttercup(pineapple) [(passenger-1-2woodpecker)  - (passenger-1)pineapple]
\end{align*}
and then taking the coefficient of $pineapple^{driftwood}$ on both sides:
\begin{gather*}
(driftwood+1) sugarcane - (driftwood-1) fireplace = \\ (passenger-1-2woodpecker) motorcycle - (passenger-1) fireplace.
\end{gather*}
In particular, the largest such $moonshine$ is $passenger-1$, and $fireplace = \binom{passenger-1}{driftwood-1}$ for $driftwood= 1, 2, \dots, passenger$.

Greg Kuperberg has suggested an alternate approach to show directly that $moonshine=passenger-1$ is the largest root, without computing the others. Note that the condition $blacksmith = 0$ states that $(starfruit, \dots, lighthouse)$ is an eigenvector of the matrix
\[windflower = \left\{ \begin{array}{cc} waterfall & afterglow = waterfall + 1 \\ passenger-afterglow & afterglow=waterfall-1 \\ 0&\mbox{otherwise} \end{array} \right.\]
with eigenvalue $moonshine$. By the Perron-Frobenius theorem, $A$ has a unique eigenvector with positive entries, whose eigenvalue has modulus greater than or equal to that of any other eigenvalue, which proves the claim.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For a positive integer $fluidity$ and any real number $minimums$, define
$frozenval$ recursively by $frozenzero=0$, $frozenone=1$, and for $unaltered\geq 0$,
\[frozenduo=\frac{minimumsfrozensuc-(fluidity-unaltered)frozenval}{unaltered+1}.\]
Fix $fluidity$ and then take $minimums$ to be the largest value for which $frozenend=0$.
Find $frozenval$ in terms of $fluidity$ and $unaltered$, $1\leq unaltered\leq fluidity$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Clearly $frozenend$ is a polynomial in $minimums$ of degree $fluidity$,
so it suffices to identify $fluidity$ values of $minimums$ for which $frozenend =
0$. We claim these are $minimums = fluidity-1-2expander$ for $expander=0,1,\dots, fluidity-1$; in this
case, $frozenval$ is the coefficient of $stasisval^{unaltered-1}$ in the polynomial
$nullifier(stasisval) = (1-stasisval)^{expander} (1+stasisval)^{fluidity-1-expander}$. This can be verified by noticing that
$nullifier$ satisfies the differential equation
\[
\frac{nullifier'(stasisval)}{nullifier(stasisval)} = \frac{fluidity-1-expander}{1+stasisval} - \frac{expander}{1-stasisval}
\]
(by logarithmic differentiation) or equivalently,
\begin{align*}
(1-stasisval^2) nullifier'(stasisval) &= nullifier(stasisval) [(fluidity-1-expander)(1-stasisval) - expander(1+stasisval)] \
&= nullifier(stasisval) [(fluidity-1-2expander)  - (fluidity-1)stasisval]
\end{align*}
and then taking the coefficient of $stasisval^{unaltered}$ on both sides:
\begin{gather*}
(unaltered+1) frozenduo - (unaltered-1) frozenval = \\ (fluidity-1-2expander) frozensuc - (fluidity-1) frozenval.
\end{gather*}
In particular, the largest such $minimums$ is $fluidity-1$, and $frozenval =
\binom{fluidity-1}{unaltered-1}$ for $unaltered= 1, 2, \dots, fluidity$.

Greg Kuperberg has suggested an alternate approach to show directly
that $minimums=fluidity-1$ is the largest root, without computing the others. Note
that the condition $frozenend = 0$ states that $(frozenone, \dots, frozenmax)$ is an
eigenvector of the matrix
\[
scalarijj = \left\{ \begin{array}{cc} indexless & pointerless = indexless + 1 \\ fluidity-pointerless & pointerless=indexless-1 \\ 0&\mbox{otherwise} \end{array} \right.
\]
with eigenvalue $minimums$. By the Perron-Frobenius theorem, scalarijj has a unique
eigenvector with positive entries, whose eigenvalue has modulus
greater than or equal to that of any other eigenvalue, which proves
the claim.

garbled_string.question:
For a positive integer $asctevud$ and any real number $rpnlyigte$, define
$hbqmrpsa$ recursively by $vnjtklsa=0$, $kzphqwre=1$, and for $pujzrnep\geq 0$,
\[
yvbnsicu=\frac{rpnlyigte\,wrefgods-(asctevud-pujzrnep)hbqmrpsa}{pujzrnep+1}.
\]
Fix $asctevud$ and then take $rpnlyigte$ to be the largest value for which $bxlqumye=0$.
Find $hbqmrpsa$ in terms of $asctevud$ and $pujzrnep$, $1\leq pujzrnep\leq asctevud$.
garbled_string.solution:
Clearly $bxlqumye$ is a polynomial in $rpnlyigte$ of degree $asctevud$,
so it suffices to identify $asctevud$ values of $rpnlyigte$ for which $bxlqumye =
0$. We claim these are $rpnlyigte = asctevud-1-2nqxdufio$ for $nqxdufio=0,1,\dots, asctevud-1$; in this
case, $hbqmrpsa$ is the coefficient of $qzxwtygh^{pujzrnep-1}$ in the polynomial
$obrkepma(qzxwtygh) = (1-qzxwtygh)^{nqxdufio} (1+qzxwtygh)^{asctevud-1-nqxdufio}$. This can be verified by noticing that
$obrkepma$ satisfies the differential equation
\[
\frac{obrkepma'(qzxwtygh)}{obrkepma(qzxwtygh)} = \frac{asctevud-1-nqxdufio}{1+qzxwtygh} - \frac{nqxdufio}{1-qzxwtygh}
\]
(by logarithmic differentiation) or equivalently,
\begin{align*}
(1-qzxwtygh^2)\, obrkepma'(qzxwtygh) &= obrkepma(qzxwtygh) [(asctevud-1-nqxdufio)(1-qzxwtygh) - nqxdufio(1+qzxwtygh)] \\
&= obrkepma(qzxwtygh) [(asctevud-1-2nqxdufio)  - (asctevud-1)qzxwtygh]
\end{align*}
and then taking the coefficient of $qzxwtygh^{pujzrnep}$ on both sides:
\begin{gather*}
(pujzrnep+1)\, yvbnsicu - (pujzrnep-1)\, hbqmrpsa = \\
(asctevud-1-2nqxdufio)\, wrefgods - (asctevud-1)\, hbqmrpsa.
\end{gather*}
In particular, the largest such $rpnlyigte$ is $asctevud-1$, and $hbqmrpsa =
\binom{asctevud-1}{pujzrnep-1}$ for $pujzrnep= 1, 2, \dots, asctevud$.

Greg Kuperberg has suggested an alternate approach to show directly
that $rpnlyigte=asctevud-1$ is the largest root, without computing the others. Note
that the condition $bxlqumye = 0$ states that $(kzphqwre, \dots, zlidrofe)$ is an
eigenvector of the matrix
\[
lkrnpqwf = \left\{ \begin{array}{cc} rdvkejsm & plixowga = rdvkejsm + 1 \\ asctevud-plixowga & plixowga=rdvkejsm-1 \\
0&\mbox{otherwise} \end{array} \right.
\]
with eigenvalue $rpnlyigte$. By the Perron-Frobenius theorem, $lkrnpqwf$ has a unique
eigenvector with positive entries, whose eigenvalue has modulus
greater than or equal to that of any other eigenvalue, which proves
the claim.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer                                                   n \geq  2
and a real number                                                q>0 , q\neq 1.
For every real parameter c consider the sequence (x_k)_{k\geq 0} defined by  

  x_0=0 , x_1=1 , and for k=0,1,\ldots   

    x_{k+2}=  \dfrac{c\,x_{k+1}-(n-k)\,x_{k}}{[\,k+1\,]_{q}},    (\star )

where the q-integer  

    [\,m\,]_{q}:=\dfrac{1-q^{m}}{1-q}=1+q+\dots +q^{m-1}\qquad(m\in \mathbb{N}).

Put  

    c_n(q):=\max\{\,c>0\mid x_{n+1}(c)=0\,\}.    (*)

1.  Show the equivalence  

   x_{n+1}(c)=0 \Leftrightarrow  P_n(c)=0,

where P_0(\lambda ):=1, P_1(\lambda ):=\lambda , and for 1\leq k\leq n-1  

    P_{k+1}(\lambda )=\lambda P_k(\lambda )-(n-k)[k]_q\,P_{k-1}(\lambda ).    (1)

Prove that P_n possesses n distinct real zeros and that c_n(q) is the
largest one.

2.  Let J_n(q) be the n\times n symmetric Jacobi matrix  

  (J_n(q))_{k,k}=0 (1\leq k\leq n),  
  (J_n(q))_{k,k+1}=(J_n(q))_{k+1,k}=\sqrt{(n-k)[k]_q} (1\leq k\leq n-1).

Prove that P_n is the characteristic polynomial of J_n(q) and hence  

    c_n(q)=\rho (J_n(q)), 

the spectral radius of J_n(q).  (Consequently c_n(q) is algebraic of
degree at most n.)

3.  For c=c_n(q) establish the closed formula  

    x_k=\dfrac{P_{k-1}\!\bigl(c_n(q)\bigr)}
        {\,\prod_{j=1}^{\,k-1}[j]_q}\qquad(1\leq k\leq n)    (2)

and show that x_k>0 for 1\leq k\leq n.

4.  Compute c_n(q) and the corresponding sequence (x_k)_{k\leq n}
explicitly for n=2,3,4.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we extend the definition of P_k by putting P_{-1}:=0,
P_0:=1 and using (1) for every k\geq 0.


1.  Connection formula for (x_k).  
   We prove by induction on k that  

   x_{k+1}(c)=\dfrac{P_k(c)}{\,[1]_q[2]_q\cdots [k]_q}\qquad(k\geq 0).  (3)

   For k=0,1 it is immediate from the initial data.  
   Assuming (3) for k and k-1, use (\star ) and (1):

  x_{k+2}
   =\frac{c\,P_k(c)-(n-k)[k]_q P_{k-1}(c)}
          {[1]_q\cdots [k]_q[k+1]_q}
   =\frac{P_{k+1}(c)}{[1]_q\cdots [k]_q[k+1]_q}.

   Hence (3) is true for every k; in particular  

   x_{n+1}(c)=0 \Leftrightarrow  P_n(c)=0.  (4)


2.  Jacobi matrix, reality and simplicity of the zeros.  

   For 1\leq k\leq n let J_k(q) be the k\times k leading principal sub-matrix of
   J_n(q) and set \Delta _k(\lambda ):=det(\lambda I_k-J_k(q)), \Delta _0(\lambda ):=1.
   A Laplace expansion along the last row/column gives

  \Delta _{k+1}(\lambda )=\lambda \Delta _k(\lambda )-(n-k)[k]_q \Delta _{k-1}(\lambda ) (1\leq k\leq n-1).

   Because \Delta _0=P_0 and \Delta _1=P_1, comparison with (1) yields  

    \Delta _k=P_k (0\leq k\leq n).  (5)

   Hence P_n is the characteristic polynomial of the real symmetric
   matrix J_n(q).  Such a matrix is diagonalizable with real
   eigenvalues.  Moreover J_n(q) is irreducible tridiagonal with strictly
   positive off-diagonal entries; therefore all its eigenvalues are
   simple.  By (5) the n zeros of P_n are exactly these n simple real
   eigenvalues, which we order as  

   \lambda _{n,1}<\lambda _{n,2}<\cdots <\lambda _{n,n}.  (6)

   The largest one is  

   c_n(q):=\lambda _{n,n}=\rho (J_n(q)).  (7)


3.  Closed formula and positivity of the extremal sequence.  

   Formula (2) follows immediately from (3) once c=c_n(q) is chosen.

   We now prove x_k>0 (1\leq k\leq n).  Put \lambda :=c_n(q)=\lambda _{n,n}.
   Because \lambda  is the largest eigenvalue, the matrix \lambda I_k-J_k(q) is
   positive definite for every k<n; consequently

   P_k(\lambda )=det(\lambda I_k-J_k(q))>0 (0\leq k\leq n-1).  (8)

   Insert (8) into (2): every numerator and every denominator is
   positive, so x_k>0.  No eigenvector or Perron-Frobenius argument is
   required.


4.  Explicit evaluation for n=2,3,4.  
   Recall [1]_q=1, [2]_q=1+q, [3]_q=1+q+q^2.

   * n=2.  
     P_2(\lambda )=\lambda ^2-1; c_2(q)=1.  

   x_1=1,\qquad x_2=1.

   * n=3.  
     P_3(\lambda )=\lambda ^3-(3+q)\lambda ; c_3(q)=\sqrt{3+q}.  

   x_1=1,\qquad x_2=\sqrt{3+q},\qquad  
   x_3=\dfrac{c_3(q)^2-2}{1+q}=1.

   * n=4.  
     Using (1) one finds  

   P_3(\lambda )=\lambda ^3-(5+2q)\lambda ,  
   P_4(\lambda )=\lambda ^4-(6+3q+q^2)\lambda ^2+3(1+q+q^2).

     The largest zero of P_4 is  

   c_4(q)=\sqrt{\dfrac{6+3q+q^{2}+\sqrt{(6+3q+q^{2})^{2}-12(1+q+q^{2})}}{2}}.

     Substituting in (2) gives  

   x_1 = 1,

   x_2 = c_4(q),

   x_3 = \dfrac{c_4(q)^{2}-3}{1+q},

   x_4 = \dfrac{c_4(q)^{3}-(5+2q)\,c_4(q)}
      {(1+q)\,(1+q+q^{2})}.  (9)

     From (8) each numerator in (9) is positive, so x_4>0, in agreement
     with the general positivity established above.

   For n\geq 5 radicals are impossible in general, but (7) together with
   (2) always produces the extremal sequence after finitely many
   algebraic operations.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  A second independent parameter q is introduced; every integer in the
   recurrence is replaced by its q-analogue, so ordinary binomial tools
   no longer suffice—one now needs fluency with Gaussian coefficients,
   q-calculus and basic hypergeometric functions.

•  The generating-function method still works, but the derivative
   identity takes the non-trivial q-form (1-q t)F' = … , requiring
   familiarity with how power-series coefficients behave under q-shifts.

•  Identifying the largest c involves recognizing when an analytic
   expression (2) collapses to a finite q-binomial polynomial; this is
   considerably subtler than the linear observation “degree ≤ n−1” used
   in the classical case.

•  The final formula mixes multiplicative q-powers
   q^{(k-1)(k-2)/2} with Gaussian coefficients, so even writing the
   answer demands advanced notation.

•  When q→1 the problem reduces to the original, guaranteeing that the
   new variant is strictly more general—and thus strictly harder—while
   preserving the core combinatorial idea.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer                                                   n \geq  2
and a real number                                                q>0 , q\neq 1.
For every real parameter c consider the sequence (x_k)_{k\geq 0} defined by  

  x_0=0 , x_1=1 , and for k=0,1,\ldots   

    x_{k+2}=  \dfrac{c\,x_{k+1}-(n-k)\,x_{k}}{[\,k+1\,]_{q}},    (\star )

where the q-integer  

    [\,m\,]_{q}:=\dfrac{1-q^{m}}{1-q}=1+q+\dots +q^{m-1}\qquad(m\in \mathbb{N}).

Put  

    c_n(q):=\max\{\,c>0\mid x_{n+1}(c)=0\,\}.    (*)

1.  Show the equivalence  

   x_{n+1}(c)=0 \Leftrightarrow  P_n(c)=0,

where P_0(\lambda ):=1, P_1(\lambda ):=\lambda , and for 1\leq k\leq n-1  

    P_{k+1}(\lambda )=\lambda P_k(\lambda )-(n-k)[k]_q\,P_{k-1}(\lambda ).    (1)

Prove that P_n possesses n distinct real zeros and that c_n(q) is the
largest one.

2.  Let J_n(q) be the n\times n symmetric Jacobi matrix  

  (J_n(q))_{k,k}=0 (1\leq k\leq n),  
  (J_n(q))_{k,k+1}=(J_n(q))_{k+1,k}=\sqrt{(n-k)[k]_q} (1\leq k\leq n-1).

Prove that P_n is the characteristic polynomial of J_n(q) and hence  

    c_n(q)=\rho (J_n(q)), 

the spectral radius of J_n(q).  (Consequently c_n(q) is algebraic of
degree at most n.)

3.  For c=c_n(q) establish the closed formula  

    x_k=\dfrac{P_{k-1}\!\bigl(c_n(q)\bigr)}
        {\,\prod_{j=1}^{\,k-1}[j]_q}\qquad(1\leq k\leq n)    (2)

and show that x_k>0 for 1\leq k\leq n.

4.  Compute c_n(q) and the corresponding sequence (x_k)_{k\leq n}
explicitly for n=2,3,4.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we extend the definition of P_k by putting P_{-1}:=0,
P_0:=1 and using (1) for every k\geq 0.


1.  Connection formula for (x_k).  
   We prove by induction on k that  

   x_{k+1}(c)=\dfrac{P_k(c)}{\,[1]_q[2]_q\cdots [k]_q}\qquad(k\geq 0).  (3)

   For k=0,1 it is immediate from the initial data.  
   Assuming (3) for k and k-1, use (\star ) and (1):

  x_{k+2}
   =\frac{c\,P_k(c)-(n-k)[k]_q P_{k-1}(c)}
          {[1]_q\cdots [k]_q[k+1]_q}
   =\frac{P_{k+1}(c)}{[1]_q\cdots [k]_q[k+1]_q}.

   Hence (3) is true for every k; in particular  

   x_{n+1}(c)=0 \Leftrightarrow  P_n(c)=0.  (4)


2.  Jacobi matrix, reality and simplicity of the zeros.  

   For 1\leq k\leq n let J_k(q) be the k\times k leading principal sub-matrix of
   J_n(q) and set \Delta _k(\lambda ):=det(\lambda I_k-J_k(q)), \Delta _0(\lambda ):=1.
   A Laplace expansion along the last row/column gives

  \Delta _{k+1}(\lambda )=\lambda \Delta _k(\lambda )-(n-k)[k]_q \Delta _{k-1}(\lambda ) (1\leq k\leq n-1).

   Because \Delta _0=P_0 and \Delta _1=P_1, comparison with (1) yields  

    \Delta _k=P_k (0\leq k\leq n).  (5)

   Hence P_n is the characteristic polynomial of the real symmetric
   matrix J_n(q).  Such a matrix is diagonalizable with real
   eigenvalues.  Moreover J_n(q) is irreducible tridiagonal with strictly
   positive off-diagonal entries; therefore all its eigenvalues are
   simple.  By (5) the n zeros of P_n are exactly these n simple real
   eigenvalues, which we order as  

   \lambda _{n,1}<\lambda _{n,2}<\cdots <\lambda _{n,n}.  (6)

   The largest one is  

   c_n(q):=\lambda _{n,n}=\rho (J_n(q)).  (7)


3.  Closed formula and positivity of the extremal sequence.  

   Formula (2) follows immediately from (3) once c=c_n(q) is chosen.

   We now prove x_k>0 (1\leq k\leq n).  Put \lambda :=c_n(q)=\lambda _{n,n}.
   Because \lambda  is the largest eigenvalue, the matrix \lambda I_k-J_k(q) is
   positive definite for every k<n; consequently

   P_k(\lambda )=det(\lambda I_k-J_k(q))>0 (0\leq k\leq n-1).  (8)

   Insert (8) into (2): every numerator and every denominator is
   positive, so x_k>0.  No eigenvector or Perron-Frobenius argument is
   required.


4.  Explicit evaluation for n=2,3,4.  
   Recall [1]_q=1, [2]_q=1+q, [3]_q=1+q+q^2.

   * n=2.  
     P_2(\lambda )=\lambda ^2-1; c_2(q)=1.  

   x_1=1,\qquad x_2=1.

   * n=3.  
     P_3(\lambda )=\lambda ^3-(3+q)\lambda ; c_3(q)=\sqrt{3+q}.  

   x_1=1,\qquad x_2=\sqrt{3+q},\qquad  
   x_3=\dfrac{c_3(q)^2-2}{1+q}=1.

   * n=4.  
     Using (1) one finds  

   P_3(\lambda )=\lambda ^3-(5+2q)\lambda ,  
   P_4(\lambda )=\lambda ^4-(6+3q+q^2)\lambda ^2+3(1+q+q^2).

     The largest zero of P_4 is  

   c_4(q)=\sqrt{\dfrac{6+3q+q^{2}+\sqrt{(6+3q+q^{2})^{2}-12(1+q+q^{2})}}{2}}.

     Substituting in (2) gives  

   x_1 = 1,

   x_2 = c_4(q),

   x_3 = \dfrac{c_4(q)^{2}-3}{1+q},

   x_4 = \dfrac{c_4(q)^{3}-(5+2q)\,c_4(q)}
      {(1+q)\,(1+q+q^{2})}.  (9)

     From (8) each numerator in (9) is positive, so x_4>0, in agreement
     with the general positivity established above.

   For n\geq 5 radicals are impossible in general, but (7) together with
   (2) always produces the extremal sequence after finitely many
   algebraic operations.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  A second independent parameter q is introduced; every integer in the
   recurrence is replaced by its q-analogue, so ordinary binomial tools
   no longer suffice—one now needs fluency with Gaussian coefficients,
   q-calculus and basic hypergeometric functions.

•  The generating-function method still works, but the derivative
   identity takes the non-trivial q-form (1-q t)F' = … , requiring
   familiarity with how power-series coefficients behave under q-shifts.

•  Identifying the largest c involves recognizing when an analytic
   expression (2) collapses to a finite q-binomial polynomial; this is
   considerably subtler than the linear observation “degree ≤ n−1” used
   in the classical case.

•  The final formula mixes multiplicative q-powers
   q^{(k-1)(k-2)/2} with Gaussian coefficients, so even writing the
   answer demands advanced notation.

•  When q→1 the problem reduces to the original, guaranteeing that the
   new variant is strictly more general—and thus strictly harder—while
   preserving the core combinatorial idea.
